data.title: The RealReal projects 9% revenue growth for 2025 amid operational efficiency gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The RealReal (REAL) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Rati Sahi Levesque outlined strong Q1 results, highlighting a GMV increase of 9% and revenue growth of 11% year-over-year. The company achieved positive adjusted EBITDA for the third consecutive quarter, amounting to $4 million.
  • The company emphasized its focus on three strategic pillars: unlocking supply through its growth playbook, driving operational efficiencies, and providing exceptional service. Initiatives such as AI-enabled programs like Athena and SmartSales were cited as key drivers of efficiency and improved consignor experience.
  • The CEO noted significant operational milestones, including a 5% increase in active buyers and a 5% rise in average order value to $564. Additionally, the number of new consignors reached its highest point in over two years.
  • CFO Ajay Gopal highlighted the 40 basis-point improvement in gross margin, which reached 75%, supported by fulfillment center efficiencies and automation efforts.
  • The CFO confirmed that direct revenue increased by 61% year-over-year, contributing to profitability improvements with gross margins of 25.5% in this segment compared to 3.3% in Q1 2024.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year 2025 guidance, projecting GMV between $1.96 billion and $1.99 billion, an 8% increase year-over-year at the midpoint. Revenue is expected to range from $645 million to $660 million, reflecting 9% year-over-year growth at the midpoint.
  • For Q2 2025, GMV is guided between $476 million and $486 million, with revenue expected to range from $157 million to $161 million, representing 9% and 10% growth, respectively, at the midpoints.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter is forecasted between $3 million and $4 million, indicating margin expansion of 350 basis points year-over-year.
  • Management noted that the direct channel is expected to contribute 10% to 15% of total revenues for the year.

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 GMV reached $490 million, a 9% increase year-over-year, while revenue totaled $160 million, up 11%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA improved to $4.1 million, a $6 million year-over-year increase.
  • Gross profit was $120 million, representing a 12% year-over-year increase, with gross margins improving by 40 basis points to 75%.
  • Operating expenses of $133 million increased by 6% year-over-year but leveraged by 410 basis points as a percentage of revenue due to cost management and AI-driven efficiencies.
  • Operating cash flow was negative $28 million, reflecting seasonality and incentive payments, but management expects improvement in the second half of the year.

Q&A

  • Ashley Owens, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Questioned the drivers and sustainability of direct revenue gross margin improvements. CFO Ajay Gopal explained the shift in direct revenue composition, highlighting the Get Paid Now initiative and improved profitability metrics.
  • Ike Boruchow, Wells Fargo: Inquired about seasonality in the direct revenue contribution and its impact on consolidated gross margins. The CFO emphasized stability in direct revenue's share of total revenues and noted its profitability enhancement.
  • Marvin Fong, BTIG: Asked about contributors to increased average order value and its components. CEO Rati Sahi Levesque attributed this to strength in fine jewelry and handbags categories.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed optimism regarding the company's operational improvements and supply initiatives, though concerns around macroeconomic headwinds and discretionary spending were raised.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly on initiatives like SmartSales and Athena, emphasizing their potential to drive efficiency and profitability.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained consistent, with ongoing confidence in the company's growth trajectory and strategic execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • GMV growth accelerated to 9% in Q1 2025 from 8% in Q4 2024, while average order value remained stable at $564.
  • Active buyer growth improved to 7% on a trailing 12-month basis, compared to 5% in Q4 2024.
  • Gross margin showed stability, with a 40 basis-point improvement year-over-year in Q1 2025, maintaining levels seen in the previous quarter.
  • Management reaffirmed its 2025 guidance with no significant deviations from the outlook provided in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential challenges from macroeconomic uncertainties but maintained that the flexibility of its business model mitigates exposure to tariffs and external pressures.
  • Analysts raised questions about discretionary spending trends and their potential impact on supply and demand dynamics.
  • Management cited AI and automation as key tools to reduce costs and improve operational resilience.

Final Takeaway

The RealReal delivered strong Q1 2025 results, driven by GMV and revenue growth, improved gross margins, and operational efficiencies. The company's focus on AI-driven initiatives like Athena and SmartSales underscores its commitment to enhancing the consignor and buyer experience while improving profitability. With reaffirmed guidance for the year and a scalable business model, management remains confident in its ability to navigate macroeconomic headwinds and achieve its 2025 objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citigroup must face $1B lawsuit claiming it aided Mexican oil company fraud - report
data.description:
Citigroup (NYSE:C) must face a reinstated lawsuit alleging it was responsible for over $1 billion in losses by facilitating and concealing a massive fraud at the now-bankrupt Mexican oil and gas services firm Oceanografía, according to a U.S. appeals court ruling reported by Reuters on Thursday.

A three-judge panel of the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Miami said 30 Oceanografia vendors, creditors, and bondholders adequately alleged that Citigroup (NYSE:C) substantially aided the fraud, and a lower court judge erred in dismissing the nine-year-old case, the report said.

Citigroup's Banamex unit had provided cash advances to Oceanografia, which provided drilling services to Mexico's state-owned oil company Petroleos Mexicanos (Pemex), and collected interest payments on the advances.

According to the report, the plaintiffs, including shipping and leasing companies, investment funds and Netherlands-based Rabobank, said Citigroup (NYSE:C) advanced $3.3 billion to Oceanografia between 2008 and 2014 despite knowing the company had too much debt and had been forging Pemex signatures on authorization forms.

data.title: Nvidia to downgrade H20 chips for China after U.S. export restrictions - report
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is set to release a downgraded version of its H20 artificial intelligence (AI) chip for the Chinese market within the next two months in response to tightened U.S. export restrictions that have blocked its sale, Reuters reported, citing sources familiar with the matter.

This move aims at maintaining the chipmaker's (NASDAQ:NVDA) presence in one of its biggest markets while complying with evolving U.S. regulations designed to limit China's access to advanced semiconductor technologies.

Nvidia (NDA) has worked on new technical specifications for the revised chip—including substantial reductions in performance, most notably a significant decrease in memory capacity and possibly other adjustments, according to the report.

China amounted to about 13% of Nvidia's total sales for the fiscal year ended January 2025. CEO Jensen Huang also visited Beijing last month to reinforce business relationships and stressed the importance of the Chinese market to the company.

Nvidia (NVDA) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

The H20 chip is central to China's AI development efforts, with customers ranging from startups like DeepSeek to tech giants such as Baidu and Alibaba.

It is likely that the downgrade is expected to make the H20 less competitive in China, potentially strengthening domestic rivals like Huawei, which already produces competing AI chips.

The U.S. government’s export controls are part of a broader effort to maintain a technological edge over China, especially in AI and semiconductor sectors. The need for a license to export the original H20 chip is the latest in a series of tightening restrictions, with both the Biden and Trump administrations supporting stricter controls.

data.title: Securitas AB (publ) reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Securitas AB (publ) press release (OTCPK:SCTBF): Q1 Total sales MSEK 39 606 (39 260)
